meta.title: Zinneke Pis | Brussels' Beloved Dog Statue - Famous Tourist Attraction
meta.description: Complete guide to Zinneke Pis in Brussels. Learn about this beloved dog statue, its history, location, and visiting information. A must-see attraction near Manneken Pis.

hero.title: Zinneke Pis
hero.subtitle: Brussels' Beloved Dog Statue & Symbol of the City's Spirit

intro.title: Discover Zinneke Pis
intro.description:
Meet Zinneke Pis, the charming dog statue that has captured the hearts of Brussels' visitors since 1998. Created by sculptor Tom Frantzen, this bronze statue depicts a dog urinating against a post — a playful sibling to the famous Manneken Pis.

Located in the charming Chartreux district, Zinneke Pis represents the rebellious and humorous spirit of Brussels. The name 'Zinneke' is a local dialect word referring to the mixed-breed dogs that once roamed the city's streets, symbolizing the cosmopolitan nature of Brussels.

Unlike its more famous human counterpart, Zinneke Pis has a devoted following among locals who appreciate its wit and the artist's tongue-in-cheek humor. The statue is often dressed in costumes during festivals, continuing the tradition established by Manneken Pis.

The surrounding neighborhood is filled with character — narrow cobblestone streets, authentic Belgian cafés, and a glimpse into the real Brussels away from tourist crowds. It's a perfect spot for those seeking authentic local experiences.

visitGuide.title: Visit Tips
items: Free to visit, no tickets required — accessible 24/7
Combine with visit to Manneken Pis (5 minutes walk) and Jeanneke Pis (7 minutes walk)
Best photographed in morning light to avoid crowds
Check for special costumes during Brussels festivals and celebrations

alsoKnownAs.title: Highlights
items: Created in 1998 by renowned Belgian sculptor Tom Frantzen
Represents the 'Zinneke' — mixed-breed dogs symbolizing Brussels' diversity
Part of the 'Pis' statue trilogy alongside Manneken and Jeanneke Pis
Located in authentic local neighborhood away from main tourist traps

sections.title: History & Creation
sections.content:
Zinneke Pis was created in 1998 by Belgian sculptor Tom Frantzen. The statue was commissioned as part of an urban renewal project in the Chartreux district. Frantzen, known for his humorous and dynamic animal sculptures, created this bronze piece that instantly became a beloved symbol of the neighborhood.

The statue depicts a dog in a natural, unposed moment — capturing the casual, irreverent spirit that defines Brussels' character. Unlike formal monuments, Zinneke Pis celebrates the everyday, the ordinary, and finds beauty in the unscripted moments of city life.

sections.title: Cultural Significance
sections.content:
The word 'Zinneke' comes from the Brussels dialect, referring to mixed-breed dogs — specifically those born from crossing city dogs with village dogs. These dogs were once common in Brussels, roaming freely between the city (the 'Zenne' river area) and surrounding villages.

Today, 'Zinneke' has become a term embracing the mixed, multicultural identity of Brussels. Just as the city brings together French and Dutch speakers, EU institutions and local traditions, Zinneke Pis celebrates this beautiful fusion. The statue has become an unofficial symbol of Brussels' cosmopolitan spirit.

sections.title: The 'Pis' Trilogy
sections.content:
Zinneke Pis completes a humorous trilogy of 'peeing' statues in Brussels:

1. Manneken Pis (1619) — the famous little boy, symbol of Brussels
2. Jeanneke Pis (1987) — the little girl, located in an alley near Manneken
3. Zinneke Pis (1998) — the dog, representing the 'common people'

Together, they represent different facets of Brussels: the official (Manneken), the feminine (Jeanneke), and the popular, down-to-earth spirit (Zinneke). Locals often joke that this trilogy perfectly captures the essence of Belgian humor.

sections.title: About the Artist
sections.content:
Tom Frantzen (born 1954) is one of Belgium's most celebrated contemporary sculptors. Known for his dynamic, expressive animal sculptures, Frantzen's works can be found throughout Brussels and beyond.

His style is characterized by movement, emotion, and often humor. Notable works include 'The Hockey Players' at the BOZAR, and numerous public sculptures. Frantzen's ability to capture the personality of animals has made him a beloved figure in Belgian art circles. Zinneke Pis remains one of his most accessible and popular works.

basicInfo.title: Basic Information
basicInfo.typeValue: Statue, Tourist Attraction, Symbol of Brussels

hours.title: Visiting Information
hours.bestTimeSpring: Morning (8-10 AM): Fewer crowds, better lighting for photos
hours.bestTimeSummer: Evening (6-8 PM): Beautiful sunset lighting, pleasant temperature
hours.bestTimeAutumn: Afternoon (2-4 PM): Soft light, comfortable for walking
hours.bestTimeWinter: Midday (11 AM-2 PM): Best natural light in winter months
hours.tip: Check for special costumes during Brussels festivals and celebrations

tickets.title: Ticket Prices
tickets.guidedPrice: No guide needed. Feel free to wander through the Chartreux district and soak in the authentic local atmosphere.
tickets.parkingPrice: Paid street parking available (~€2-3/hour). Recommended to park at Interparking Bourse or use public transport.

transport.title: How to Get Here
transport.fromCenterDesc: Walk 10 minutes from Grand Place through the charming streets of Brussels. Follow Rue au Beurre, then Rue des Chartreux. The statue is located in a small square.
transport.fromStationDesc: Walk 12 minutes from the station. Exit towards Rue de l'Impératrice, walk straight to Grand Place, then follow signs to Chartreux district.
transport.publicTransportDesc: Take metro lines 1 or 5 to 'De Brouckère' station, then walk 8 minutes. Or take bus 48 to 'Bourse' stop, then walk 5 minutes.
transport.walkingDesc: Perfect addition to Brussels walking tour — combine with Manneken Pis (5 min), Jeanneke Pis (7 min), and Grand Place (10 min).
transport.drivingDesc: Parking is limited in the area. Recommended to park at 'Interparking Bourse' or use public transport. The area is pedestrian-friendly.
transport.fromAirportDesc: Take a direct train (~20 mins) to Brussels Central Station, then walk 12 minutes to the statue's location.

route.title: Recommended Walking Route
route.overview: Start at Grand Place, walk to Manneken Pis (5 min), then to Zinneke Pis (5 min), continue to Jeanneke Pis (7 min), and explore the charming Chartreux district. The entire route takes about 2 hours including photo stops and café breaks.
steps: Start at Grand Place, admire the magnificent Town Hall and Guildhalls
Walk to Manneken Pis via Rue au Beurre (5 minutes)
Take photos with the famous little boy statue, check his costume schedule
Continue to Zinneke Pis in Chartreux district (5 minutes)
Enjoy the charming dog statue, take creative photos
Walk to Jeanneke Pis in the alley (7 minutes)
Complete the 'Pis Trilogy' with the little girl statue
Explore the authentic Chartreux neighborhood, visit local cafés
Walk back to Grand Place via Rue des Bouchers (10 minutes)
End with Belgian waffles or beer at a terrace on Grand Place

spots.name: Side Angle
spots.desc: Profile view highlighting the dynamic pose and sculptor's attention to anatomical details

spots.name: With the Post
spots.desc: Including the lamppost in frame, showing the complete composition as artist intended

spots.name: Neighborhood Context
spots.desc: Step back to capture the statue within its charming local neighborhood setting

spots.name: Creative Angles
spots.desc: Get creative! Locals love taking fun, unconventional photos with Zinneke

reviews.title: Visitor Reviews
reviews.declaration: Visitor feedback can be viewed via Google Maps (external link).
reviews.moreReviews: View more reviews on Google Maps

items.name: Marie Dubois
items.text: Absolutely charming! Much less crowded than Manneken Pis. Love the rebellious spirit and the neighborhood is so authentic. A hidden gem in Brussels!

items.name: John Smith
items.text: Discovered this by accident while wandering. What a delightful surprise! The dog statue has so much character. Don't miss it if you're in Brussels.

items.name: Anna Müller
items.text: Cute statue in a lovely local area. It's free and open anytime. We combined it with visits to Manneken and Jeanneke Pis - makes for a fun walking tour!

items.name: Pieter Van Dam
items.text: As a local, Zinneke is my favorite of the three 'Pis' statues. It represents the real Brussels - mixed, multicultural, and with a great sense of humor.

officialManagement.title: About Zinneke Pis
officialManagement.text:
Zinneke Pis is a beloved bronze statue created by Belgian sculptor Tom Frantzen in 1998. Located in the authentic Chartreux district of Brussels, it represents the rebellious and humorous spirit of the city.

The statue is part of Brussels' cultural heritage, symbolizing the cosmopolitan and mixed identity of the city. 'Zinneke' refers to the mixed-breed dogs that once roamed Brussels, becoming an unofficial symbol of the city's diversity and multicultural spirit.
